instructions.description:
## Intro
This is a simple applet showcasing the power of the [rumtk](https://github.com/MedicalMasses-L-L-C/rumtk) framework. The goal of this applet is to provide a simple interface for running different kinds of benchmarks on the framework. I also hope to add an export feature to the applet so the results can be saved to disk for record keeping.

This utility is not specific to the [rumtk](https://github.com/MedicalMasses-L-L-C/rumtk) toolset. You can configure your own profiles to examine your own programs. This benchmarking tool might not be appropriate for interpreted programs since we look at the binary's call stack. As a result, I suspect that the only useful information you might obtain from benchmarking an interpreted program is statistics about the interpreter itself.

The benchmark report is broken down into these sections:
* `Meta`: A small section showing the target architecture and OS of this `rumtk-benchmark` build.
* `File List`: list of test files and their sizes in MB used for running test of utility.
* `Basic`: A simple run of the selected utility using `hyperfine`. The resulting report shows the mean times with breakdowns by user and kernel spaces.
* `Flamegraph`: Interactive flamegraph attempting to visualize call graph of the test utility under load. Helpful in identifying function targets of interest for future optimizations. This visualization is generated by performing a quick benchmark with the `perf` command and then passing the report to [flamegraph-rs](https://github.com/flamegraph-rs/flamegraph).
* `CPU Summary`: Simple summary report of basic CPU statistics obtained with the `perf` command.
* `CPU Cache & Branch Misses`: Report of functions showing a % cache and branch miss of > 1%. This helps with identifying if the performance issue is coming from a large number of CPU cache misses. Meaning, if the program is not maximizing the usage of the data already present in the CPU cache lines or a failure of the program to pre-train the CPU algorithm to prefetch necessary data. You do get a technical subreport of the current 

Information that is not collected:
* `O Analysis`: Currently, we do not gather the information necessary to analyze the general behavior of the program or algorithm. Therefore, this benchmark utility does not provide big O notation information.
* `SIMD`: Currently, this utility does not inspect and present a dissassembly of the test program and in particular the key functions. Thus, no information about proper usage of **SIMD** extensions can be ascertain. I recommend you use Matt Goldbolt's excellent [Compiler Explorer](https://godbolt.org/) tool or `objdump` for that.

## Contributing
If you appreciate the work done in this project, please, visit the following links:
* [OpenCollective](https://opencollective.com/medicalmasses-llc).
* Navigate to [MedicalMasses L.L.C.'s](https://medicalmasses.com) website.
* Or reach out to [Luis's email](mailto:lsantos@medicalmasses.com).
